observations.recommendation:
1.12 We recommended and Management agreed to:
1.12.a Stop the utilization of trust funds for purposes other than for their specific purposes; and
1.12.b Implement stricter controls requiring that all cash advances be supported by clearly identified and approved fund sources, with corresponding appropriation or trust fund documentation, prior to the grant of cash advance.

1.17 We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure that expenses incurred through cash advances are recorded in the period in which they were actually incurred, regardless of when the liquidation reports are submitted. This will promote compliance with the accrual basis of accounting and ensure the fair and accurate presentation of account balances in the financial statements as of yearend.

2.9 We recommended and Management agreed to:
2.9.a Require the immediate refund of unutilized balances of cash advances totaling ₱4,410.00 from the accountable officers concerned;
2.9.b Enforce strict compliance with COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 2012-001 by ensuring that all cash advances are liquidated promptly and fully, and any unused amounts are refunded and officially receipted immediately upon submission of the liquidation; and
2.9.c Review and strengthen internal procedures for processing liquidations to prevent recurrence, and strictly enforce the prohibition against granting additional cash advances to any officer with outstanding unliquidated balances, pursuant to Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

3.9 We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the DPWH and immediately secure the necessary documents, such as turnover reports, project completion certificates, and property transfer documentation, to facilitate the recording of the completed building and water supply system, along with the corresponding accumulated depreciation, in the LGU’s books to ensure compliance with COA accounting policies and fair presentation of the financial statements. The following is the proposed adjusting journal entry (AJE):
Account Title Debit Credit
Buildings 4,882,916.21
Water Supply Systems 33,320,000.00
Accumulated Depreciation – Buildings xxx
Accumulated Depreciation – Water Supply
Systems xxx
Government Equity xxx
To record the completed projects implemented by
the DPWH

4.8 We recommended that Management:
4.8.a Provide a written justification for the use of the trust fund to acquire a motorcycle, when it was specifically appropriated for the procurement of furniture and fixtures, an expenditure that clearly deviated from the original and authorized purpose of the fund; and
4.8.b Henceforth, ensure that all disbursements from trust funds are made strictly in accordance with their specific and authorized purpose, as required under Section 305(a) of RA 7160 and COA regulations, to uphold the principles of accountability, transparency, and lawful use of public funds.

6.12 We recommended and Management agreed that:
6.12.a Management provide justification for the utilization of the LDRRMF for the repair of the PNP vehicle and the purchase of laptop;
6.12.b The Budget Officer and Accountant ensure that all future obligations are accurately charged in accordance with their proper budget classification and fund source; and
6.12.c The Budget Officer, Accountant, and DRRMO strengthen controls and review procedures to ensure that future disbursements are consistent with the intended purpose of the DRRM fund, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 and other applicable regulations.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer enforce immediate and full collection of disallowances that have become final and executory, pursuant to the COA Order of Execution (COE) and Section 7.1.3 of the RRSA.

1.10 We have recommended and Management agreed that:
The Municipal Accountant consider taking up the second proposed adjusting entry in Table 2 to reconcile the intra-agency accounts and to enable the elimination of the accounts in   succeeding preparation of consolidated financial statements; and
The Municipal Treasurer cause the fund transfer of intra-agency funds to their appropriate bank accounts and strictly monitor the preparation of check payments to avoid erroneous charging of disbursements to other accounts and the eventual bloating of the intra-agency accounts.

2.7 	We have recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Consider the following adjusting journal entry to re-classify the unfinished buildings to the construction in progress account:

Henceforth, record unfinished structures under the Construction in Progress account which are for reclassification to the appropriate PPE account upon completion.

4.6 	We have recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to recognize income on the year when the income was collected and not on the year it was submitted to ensure fair presentation of income in the financial statements.

5.5 	We have recommended and Management agreed that:
The Municipal Accountant check and determine if all prior years’ 20% DF allocations were transferred from the GF account to the 20% DF bank account.
The Finance Committee work out for the identification of the difference and the eventual reconciliation between the 20% DF balance in the SAAOB of unimplemented projects and unpaid obligations and the Cash In Bank- 20% DF.

We have recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to immediately cause the updating of the passbooks of the time deposit accounts and prepare and submit to the Audit Team the Bank Reconciliation Statements together with the passbooks/bank statements of the time deposits in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-011; and prepare the necessary journal entry to recognize the interest income earned for the period February 2021 to December 31, 2023.

8.9 	We have recommended and Management agreed to:
Henceforth, impose the immediate liquidation of cash advances pursuant to Section 89 of PD 1445;
Refrain from granting additional cash advances to accountable officers unless their previous cash advances are liquidated or a proper accounting thereof has been made; and
In case of excessive cash advance, ensure that the accountable officer refund immediately any excess thereof.

9.9 	We have recommended and Management agree to ensure that:
A periodic physical count on inventory items by type is conducted semestrally;
The General Service Officer prepare and submit duly accomplished Report on the Physical Count of Inventories, maintain stock cards to record purchases and issuances of inventories, reconcile the same with the ledger card of the accounting office, and update the same regularly; and
The Municipal Accounting Office to use the perpetual inventory method of accounting supplies and materials; maintain Supplies Ledger Cards per stock to be reconciled with the stock cards of the GSO to ensure fair presentation of inventory accounts in the financial statements; and ensure that all purchases are supported with properly accomplished Requisition and Issue Slips and Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued to support charges to inventory expense accounts at any given period.

10.7 	We have recommended and Management agreed to require the:
MDRRMO to submit the soonest the distribution list/s for the above-mentioned relief goods amounting to ₱119,290.00 and for succeeding relief goods distribution; and
Accounting Office to record the relief goods as inventory upon purchase and to record the expense upon the receipt of the duly accomplished RIS supported by a distribution list signed by the beneficiaries.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a.	require the Municipal Accountant to comply with COA Circular No. 2016-005 by reconciling and cleaning of the Loans Receivable account for the fair presentation of the Municipality’s Financial Statements; and

b.	enforce repayment of the Loan from the Mayoyao Weavers Association in order to pay the Municipality’s loan to the Presidential Management Staff.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the
completed project in the books by debiting Road Networks and crediting
Government Equity accounts amounting to P2,778,726.78 for the fair
presentation of the financial statements of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to, henceforth, keep abreast of the disclosure requirements in
the PPSAS and COA Circulars in order to provide adequate and relevant
information for the fair presentation of the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to comply with COA Circular No.
2016-005 by reconciling and cleaning of the Loans Receivable account for
the fair presentation of the Municipalitys Financial Statements; and
b. enforce repayment of the Loan from the Mayoyao Weavers Association in
order to pay the Municipalitys loan to the Presidential Management Staff.

Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to remit to the Bureau of the
Treasury the unexpended balance and liquidated damages totalling
P312,964.61.

Accountant to prepare the adjusting journal entry to correct the
overstatement of the Trust Liability-DRRMF account amounting to
32,784.74. Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant
adhere to the LDRRMF accounting guidelines provided under COA
Circular No. 2012-002.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to enforce immediate and full collection of disallowances which
have become final and executory, pursuant to the COEs and Section 7.1.3
of the RRSA.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to determine the composition/breakdown of the unremitted
withheld premiums of P66,778.95 for GSIS, P39,297.13 for PHIC and
P21,486.11 for Pag-ibig and immediately remit these to the concerned
agency, where applicable. In addition, we recommended that the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure that the exact amount of
future premiums/loan amortizations are withheld and promptly remitted to
these government-owned and controlled corporations.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant record the ₱2,195,769.17 total disallowances that became final
and executory in CY 2017, and maintain the proper subsidiary ledger (SL)
for each of the persons liable, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-
006.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant recognize in the books the operations of the loach hatchery and
the rubber tree nursery having met the definition and recognition criteria
of biological assets provided under PPSAS 27 – Agriculture.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant cause the recognition of unrecorded public infrastructures, so
that by the end of 2018, at least 75% have been recorded back to the book
of accounts in compliance with the adoption of PPSAS.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant recognize PPEs that were recorded as "Repairs and
Maintenance" under the MDRRMF totaling ₱1,612,406.39, and PPEs
constructed and purchased out of the Trust Funds totaling ₱13,406,410.00;
and, henceforth, recognize as PPE those that qualify as such under PPSAS
17 and transfer to the General Fund all assets constructed and purchased
out of the Trust Fund.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant adjust the “Road Networks” and “Government Equity”
accounts for the ₱900,000.00 overstatement, adjust the SLs accordingly,
and change the SL names to the specific names of the municipal roads.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant adjust the affected accounts; and, henceforth, diligently
monitor the payable accounts to properly record payment of liabilities,
observe the PPSAS recognition criteria for PPEs, and provide depreciation
expense for each of the PPE under the 20% Municipal Development Fund.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant adjust the affected accounts; provide the depreciation expense;
and group under the proper SLs all the recorded DRRM-related
equipment according to their use.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant (a) recognize in the “Information and Communication
Technology Equipment” account the additional ₱10,000.00 cost, reclassify
to the correct SL, and provide the correct depreciation of the purchased
laptop; (b) adjust the “Due to NGAS” and “Due from Local Government
Units” accounts for ₱744,430.49 and ₱969,852.51, respectively; and, (c)
henceforth, recognize as “Due from Local Government Units” all fund
transfers to barangays, and apply PPSAS when recording transactions
involving General Fund counterparts to the Trust Fund.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant apply the PPSAS whenever a program/project/activity is
funded by both the General and Trust Funds, and adjust the “Trust
Liabilities-Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund,” “Due to/from
Other Funds” and the affected Government Equity sub-accounts to reflect
their actual and correct balances.

We have recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the MDRRMF Council make appropriate corrective action on the
negative balance of the CY 2013 Special Trust Fund; and
b. the Municipal Accountant: (i) establish the correct balances of the
individual Special Trust Funds as of December 31, 2017 as basis for the
preparation of the CY 2018 MDRRMFIP by the MDRRM
Council/MDRRMO, after incorporating the corrective action in
recommendation “a” above; and (ii) obtain a copy of the detailed list of
programs and activities funded under the Special Trust Funds per year
of transfer to aid in the fund tracking of charges and balances of each
Special Trust Fund; record expenses cautiously in the correct SLs to
avoid unreliable balances; and revert back to the General Fund the
Special Trust Fund not fully utilized after five (5) years, if any, after
effecting all corrections in the balances.

We have recommended and Management agreed that (a) the Municipal
Accountant, henceforth, observe the reporting cut-off date in recording,
especially, the year-end transactions; and (b) the Municipal Budget Officer
record the obligations for completed projects as of the reporting cut-off
date to facilitate the recording thereof in the accounting books.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements, information on
DRRM-related inventories and PPE, and the reconciliation of the balance
of the “Trust Liabilities-Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund”
account at the beginning and end of the period, in compliance with COA
Circular No. 2014-002 and PPSAS 1.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant prepare the Trust Fund financial statements based on the
general ledger account balances at the reporting date.

We have recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant include all the components of the Cash and Cash Equivalents
account in the preparation of the Statement of Cash Flows in compliance
with PPSAS 2 – Cash Flow Statements.

We have recommended and Management agreed:
a. that the Municipal Accountant impose the 10% Retention Money from
progress payments of all infrastructure projects in accordance with the
guidelines in RA No. 9184;
b. to require contractors to post Performance Security, in whatever form,
with validity period covering contract signing until date of final
acceptance;
c. to require the contractors to post Warranty Security, in whatever form,
as required in RA No. 9184;
d. to strengthen the monitoring and inspection mechanisms of the MLGU,
and proceed with the issuance of the Certificate of Completion after
thorough evaluation of the projects as required, and the Certificate of
Final Acceptance after one (1) year from the date of Certificate of
Completion to cover the defects liability period; and
e. to make officials concerned liable incase defects in projects not imposed
with required warranties, and where contractors refuse to rectify.

We have recommended and Management agreed to immediately cause the
settlement of the long outstanding disallowances of former employees
either by collecting directly from them, or by collecting from the persons
solidarily liable.

We have recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the MDRRM Council appropriate the MDRRMF only for disaster risk
management activities, support to other affected LGUs and quick
response fund or stand-by fund for relief and recovery programs, as
contemplated in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-01 and COA
Circular No. 2012-002, and stop the lump-sum appropriation for the
rehabilitation/improvement/restoration of public infrastructures;
b. the MDRRM Officer submit report on the evaluation of the public
infrastructures totaling ₱1,010,351.89 that were
rehabilitated/improved/restored due to heavy rains, typhoons and other
calamity; and
c. the Municipal Budget Officer, henceforth, charge the assistance to
emergency responders against the General Fund for the related public
gathering and special occasion as it is an administrative expense.

We have recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, prepare,
based on correct RAOs, the monthly Report on the Receipt and
Utilization of the DRRMF sourced from GAA beginning CY 2018; and
b. the Municipal Budget Officer, adjust the CYs 2015-2017 Preparedness
and Mitigation-CO RAOs to reflect the correct balances, and the CY
2017 Preparedness and Mitigation-MOOE to correct the amount that
should be transferred to the Special Trust Fund; and, henceforth,
cautiously charge the obligations to the related allotment to produce a
monthly Report on the Receipt and Utilization of the DRRMF sourced
from GAA that is based on correct RAOs.

We have recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the Municipal Engineer ensure that infrastructure projects are
completed within the target period of completion;
b. the Municipal Development Council review and re-evaluate the
feasibility of long overdue 20% MDF projects and revert the
appropriations of those no longer feasible (overtaken by time and the
changes in the present needs of beneficiaries) for implementation of
other priority development projects;
c. the MDRRM Council re-appropriate to DRRM-related programs those
administrative expenses funded under MDRRMF (Annex H), and the
MDRRM Officer expedite the implementation of the programs under
the MDRRMF capital outlays identified to reduce and manage disaster
risks; and
d. the Heads of Office concerned, (i) expedite the implementation of fund
transfers in accordance with the purpose specified in the relative
Memorandum of Agreement (MOA), if still possible; or (ii) return to
the source agency the unutilized/idle fund transfers, if no provision in
the MOA for the use of remaining balances.

We have recommended that Management complete the Tax Mapping Operations, through the Municipal Assessor’s Office, by providing them adequate funds, modern equipment, manpower and other needed support, for them to come up with reliable RPT records that will establish a permanent link between the real properties in the field and the property assessment and tax records in the office. Alongside, effectively educate constituents on the merits of a rational and responsive real property assessment and appraisal system through tax mapping. Thereafter, ensure that reports submitted to the Municipal Treasurer, which are forwarded to the Municipal Accountant, are accurate and reliable so that the tax burden will be distributed equitably, and proper accounting thereof be established.
observations.managementAction: Funds were provided for the purchase of software from Geodata Systems, Inc. (Geodata Systems).
observations.reason: Last tax mapping operations were in 2008 where identification of lots was not maximized Geodata Systems focuses on providing the geographic information system (GIS) and mapping solutions in the Philippines, and the sole authorized distributor of the mapping and spatial analytics software from Esri. 50% of the Municipality was mapped. No approved tax declarations so far.

observations.managementAction: The disallowance was recorded in the individual subsidiary ledgers of the persons liable.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant, adjust the “Receivables-Disallowances/Charges” account to bring the affected subsidiary ledger accounts to their normal debit balances by transferring the negative balances to the “Accounts Payable” account; and to ensure that the “Receivables-Disallowances/Charges” account is correctly stated by diligently monitoring its sub-accounts.
observations.managementAction: The “Receivables-Disallowances/Charges” account was adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that (a) revisit, through the Municipal Accountant, the public infrastructure projects completed in CY 2016 that were recognized under “Repairs and Maintenance” accounts, and with the assistance of the Municipal Engineer, identify those that qualify for recognition as PPE under PPSAS 17, for adjustment and proper classification under the sub-accounts of the Water Supply Systems,” and “Other Infrastructure Assets” account; and (b) conduct inventory, through the Inventory Committee, of infrastructure projects funded by the Municipality to be included in the inventory list of PPE which is to be reconciled with those recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructures.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management recognize, through the Municipal Accountant and with the help of other heads of offices, and under PPSAS those public infrastructures previously recognized under NGAS, so that at the end of 2017 at least 50% have been recorded back to the book of accounts in compliance with COA Circular No. 2016-004.

observations.reason: Road networks from CY 2011 and prior years, and other components of the infrastructure assets from CYs 2015 and prior years were not recorded.

observations.managementAction: In CY 2017, Management complied with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular 2011-1.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management, through the Municipal Engineer: a. compute the corresponding liquidated damages for the above completed projects and submit to the Accountant for the deduction of the same from the retention fees or any amount due the contractors; b. henceforth, devise ways for the adequate supervision and monitoring of all infrastructure projects being implemented under his office; and c. provide complete data in the supporting documents, i.e. contract duration and percentage of accomplishment on the date of contract expiry.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineering Office reviewed the billing documents of the project and found that a letter request from the contractor for the extension of 30 days was granted. The Municipal Engineering Office supervised and monitored the substantial implementation of all the infrastructure projects and supporting documents for the computation of liquidated damages were attached.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management explain and justify the deviation from the provisions of Section 52 of RA No. 10717.
observations.managementAction: Claims of persons liable were used to partially settle the disallowance through monthly payroll deduction. Settlement of the disallowance was recorded under the “Other Payables” account.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant requested to wait for the decision of this Office on his appeal. This was in reference to the disallowance on the irregular payment of foliar fertilizer due to non-conformity with Section 10, Article IV of RA No. 9184, which became final and executory on January 14, 2010. A COA Order of Execution (COE) was issued on October 28, 2015, in which the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant were part of those persons liable.

Auditor’s Rejoinder
It can be gleaned from the foregoing that the zero net take home pay was to avoid the COE.
Since a COE has been issued, settlement of the disallowance is mandatory, which can be recorded under the “Accounts Payable” account while the result of the appeal is still pending.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management: a. realign funds only for the augmentation of items in the approved annual budget for the respective offices from savings in other items within the same expense class of their respective appropriations in compliance with Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160, otherwise, funds shall be available exclusively for the specific purpose for which they have been appropriated; b. strictly comply with the procedural requirements of Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160 in realigning funds by making an efficient and effective planning and budgeting to incorporate all approved expenditure items most likely to be incurred in the annual operations of the Municipality taking into consideration the experiences and knowledge acquired from the long stint in the government service; and c. explain why the disbursements charged against the realigned funds, in violation of Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160, should not be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2017, Management made sure that realignments were only made to augment existing items in the approved annual budget.
observations.reason: Management promised to realign only for the augmentation of existing items in the approved annual budget.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management, through the Municipal Budget Officer, cause transfer of funds from one item to another in the RAOs only when there is an ordinance authorizing the realignment of funds, in compliance with Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160; and explain why the disbursements charged against the realigned funds, in violation of Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160, should not be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2017, realignments were only made in pursuance with ordinances authorizing the realignment of funds.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management submit the reports and documents related to the two (2) transportation equipment disposed thru public bidding in CY 2016, and, whenever there are properties assessed for disposal, apply in this Office for their inspection and appraisal.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted the required reports and documents.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management: a. follow-up the approval of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan, and thereafter, request for funding of relevant projects from the National SWM Fund in accordance with the NSWMC guidelines; b. continue to assist the remaining 24 barangays in the establishment of their MRF; c. update this Office on the result of the evaluation made by the EMB on the Municipality’s compliance with the closure of its open dumpsite; d. continue to enhance awareness of constituents on SWM and develop stricter and more effective implementation techniques of SWM; and e. through the Sangguniang Bayan, expedite the passing of pending ordinances relative to SWM that will ensure the protection of public health and environment.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan was approved, and funding was requested but not available due to insufficient funds in the National SWM Fund. Assistance to nine (9) barangays was given in CY 2018. Funding for the closure of the open dumpsite was provided in the Municipality’s CY 2017 Supplemental Budget. IEC campaigns and trainings on SWM are continuously being conducted, and the compliance with SWM of the community is closely monitored. A separate SWM Fund was set-up in CY 2018 for the implementation of the Solid Waste Management Plan. Municipal ordinances not consistent with the recently approved SWM Plan are being amended.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management: a. consider the quantity and nature of development projects being proposed for inclusion in the budget, so that these are proportionate to the capacity of available resources in the implementation of the projects, especially the manpower resources of the Municipal Engineering Office; b. through the Municipal Planning and development Coordinator, evaluate the technical viability of proposed development projects before these are being requested for appropriation to avoid realignments; and c. strategize with the Municipal Engineering Office and other offices concerned to fast track the implementation of projects.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2017, the Municipal Development Council came up with list of priority projects which is 70% less in quantity than that of the previous year, through the recommendation of the Municipal Engineering Office. Proposed development projects in CY 2017 were technically viable. Development projects including those from previous years were substantially implemented.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management, henceforth, coordinate with the MSB to maximize the utilization of the SEF to meet the existing and growing demands of basic education services, which the fund intends to provide. Further, to promptly update the MSB, through the Municipal Treasurer, of availability of funds for appropriation.
observations.managementAction: Management coordinated with the MSB to maximize the utilization of the SEF. From CY 2016 to CY 2017, there was an increase of 54% in the utilization of the total appropriation.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management: a. send the newly designated GAD Focal Point to trainings on the understanding of GAD; b. require the GAD Focal Point to administer the HGDG test on the LGU’s GAD PPAs in the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget. c. administer the HGDG test on its PPAs for CY 2017, the results of which shall be the basis in estimating the cost of gender mainstreaming and GAD Budget; d. if and when necessary, conduct a training program on the HGDG for the incoming year and seek assistance of a gender analysis expert in the use of and/administration of the HGDG; and e. expedite the implementation of GAD PPAs to accelerate gender mainstreaming in the MLGU operations.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Point attended the “Training on Assessing the Gender Responsiveness of BUB/ADM Funded Projects” held on November 23-24, 2017 by the DILG.
observations.reason: The HGDG tests were administered on the CY 2018 GAD Plan and Budget. The HGDG tests were not administered in CY 2017.

and include in the CY 2017 MDRRMFIP the list of PPAs to be charged under

the funds to avoid audit disallowance; and, in the re-appropriation of the
MDRRMF to new expenditure items, an approved revised AIP in accordance
with the approved MDRRMFIP is required.

infrastructure projects being implemented under his office; and
c. provide complete data in the supporting documents, i.e. contract duration and
percentage of accomplishment on the date of contract expiry.

We have recommended that Management explain and justify the deviation from
the provisions of Section 52 of RA No. 10717.

We have recommended and Management agreed to:
a. realign funds only for the augmentation of items in the approved annual
budget for the respective offices from savings in other items within the same
expense class of their respective appropriations in compliance with Section
336 of R.A. No. 7160, otherwise, funds shall be available exclusively for the
specific purpose for which they have been appropriated;
b. strictly comply with the procedural requirements of Section 336 of R.A. No.
7160 in realigning funds by making an efficient and effective planning and
budgeting to incorporate all expenditure items most likely to be incurred in
the annual operations of the Municipality taking into consideration the
experiences and knowledge acquired from the long stint in the government
service; and
c. explain why the disbursements charged against the realigned funds, in
violation of Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160, should not be disallowed in audit.

We have recommended and Management agreed to:
a. advise the Municipal Budget Officer to cause transfer of funds from one item
to another in the RAOs only when there is an ordinance authorizing the
realignment of funds, in compliance with Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160; and
b. explain why the disbursements charged against the realigned funds, in
violation of Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160, should not be disallowed in audit.

We have recommended and Management agreed to submit the reports and
documents related to the two (2) transportation equipment disposed thru public
bidding in CY 2016, and, whenever there are properties assessed for disposal, to
apply in this Office for their inspection and appraisal.

We have recommended and Management agreed to:
a. follow-up the approval of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan, and
thereafter, request for funding of relevant projects from the National SWM
Fund in accordance with the NSWMC guidelines;
b. continue to assist the remaining 24 barangays in the establishment of their
MRF;

Municipality’s compliance with the closure of its open dumpsite;
d. continue to enhance awareness of constituents on SWM and develop stricter
and more effective implementation techniques of SWM guidelines and
ordinances; and
e. through the Sanggunian Bayan, expedite the passing of pending ordinances
relative to SWM that will ensure the protection of public health and
environment.

We have recommended and Management agreed to assess the probability of
collection of the long outstanding disallowances of former employees and
strategize on ways for their immediate collection; and to cause the collection of
disallowances from personnel solidarily liable when collection from the former
employees, who are primarily liable, is unachievable.

We have recommended and Management agreed to:
a. consider the quantity and nature of development projects being proposed for
inclusion in the budget, so that these are proportionate to the capacity of
available resources in the implementation of projects, especially the
manpower resources of the Municipal Engineering Office;
b. through the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, evaluate the
technical viability of proposed development projects before these are being
requested for appropriation to avoid realignments; and
c. strategize with the Municipal Engineering Office and other offices concerned
to fast track the implementation of projects.

economic services to the constituents of the Municipality; and, through the

We have recommended and Management agreed to, henceforth, coordinate with
the MSB to maximize the utilization of the SEF to meet the existing and growing

Further, to promptly update the MSB, through the Municipal Treasurer, of
availability of funds for appropriation.

We have commended Management for its continued commitment and support in
helping the government generate funds through prompt remittance of all taxes
withheld.

We have recommended and Management agreed to:
a. send the newly designated GAD Focal Point to trainings on the understanding
of GAD;
b. require the GAD Focal Point to administer the HGDG test on the LGU’s GAD
PPAs in the preparation of Accomplishment Report, as basis for determining
the actual expenditure that can be attributed to the GAD Budget;
c. administer the HGDG test on its PPAs for CY 2017, the results of which shall
be the basis in estimating the cost of gender mainstreaming and GAD Budget;
d. if and when necessary, conduct a training program on the HGDG for the
incoming year and seek assistance of a gender analysis expert in the use of
and/administration of the HGDG; and
e. expedite the implementation of GAD PPAs to accelerate gender
mainstreaming in the MLGU operations.

We have recommended and Management agreed to, henceforth, require all
elected officials and heads of offices to undergo capacity-building programs on
GAD. For economy, invite competent speakers and conduct in-house training to
maximize the participation of the focal point system, elected officials, heads of
offices, employees and other stakeholders, i.e. Barangay officials, to harmonize
GAD-related activities in the ensuing year. Such capacity building programs on
GAD shall include, but not limited to: gender sensitivity training, gender-
responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis, gender audit and GAD tools
such as the Gender Mainstreaming Evaluation Framework, Gender Responsive
LGU Ka Ba Self-Assessment Tool, Harmonized Gender and Development
Guidelines and others.
